Professional bio

My journey in AI/ML

I'm Ajala Olakunle, a Artificial Intelligence student. My journey into AI began with a simple question: how can machines learn to make better decisions than they were explicitly programmed to? That curiosity quickly became a passion and eventually a career path.

My academic journey has taken me through the full spectrum of machine learning: from classical supervised and unsupervised algorithms, to deep neural networks, to the frontier of generative AI. I've built models on tabular data, trained CNNs on image datasets, fine-tuned transformer models for NLP tasks, and explored diffusion models for image generation.

Beyond the technical work, I care deeply about why we build AI systems the ethical considerations, the societal impact, and the importance of explainability. I believe the best AI practitioners are not just strong engineers but thoughtful communicators who can bridge the gap between complex models and real-world stakeholders.

I'm currently seeking internship and research opportunities where I can apply my skills to meaningful challenges in healthcare, climate science, or financial services. My goal is to become an ML engineer who ships models that genuinely improve people's lives.

Portfolio artifacts

Six artifacts that define my work

Each entry follows the standard artifact template: title, introduction, description, objective, process, tools, and value proposition.

🗺️
Artifact 01
ML Algorithms Visual Framework
A comprehensive visual infographic classifying 10 key machine learning algorithms by learning type, application domain, and real-world use case — designed as a reference tool for AI/ML students and practitioners.
Supervised Learning Unsupervised Learning Deep Learning Reinforcement Learning NLP Generative AI

Description

An interactive visual framework organizing 10 major ML algorithms Random Forest, XGBoost, Decision Trees, SVMs, K-Means, PCA, CNNs, BERT, GPT, GANs, Diffusion Models, and Q-Learning — across four primary AI/ML domains. Each algorithm entry includes a plain-language explanation, learning type classification, and real-world use cases.

Objective

To build and demonstrate a deep, comparative understanding of the machine learning algorithm landscape showing not just what each algorithm does, but when and why to use it over alternatives. The artifact serves as both a learning artifact and a reference document for algorithm selection in real-world projects.

Process

  • Reviewed course materials and external resources on AI, ML, DL, and GenAI distinctions
  • Categorized 10 algorithms by learning paradigm (supervised, unsupervised, reinforcement, deep learning)
  • Identified primary application domains for each: tabular data, computer vision, NLP, generative AI
  • Researched real-world use cases and industry applications for each algorithm
  • Designed and built an interactive visual framework with color-coded classification system
  • Added the AI → ML → DL → GenAI hierarchy diagram to contextualize relationships

Tools & Technologies

HTML / CSS JavaScript Claude AI e-Core / Stanford AI resources Google Fonts
Interactive Algorithm Visual Framework
🤖
AI
Artificial Intelligence — machines that simulate human intelligence
→
📊
ML
Machine Learning — systems that learn from data
→
🧠
DL
Deep Learning — multi-layer neural networks
→
✨
GenAI
Generative AI — models that create new content
🌳 Random Forest
Supervised
Builds hundreds of decision trees on random subsets of the data, then combines their predictions by majority vote (classification) or averaging (regression) "wisdom of the crowd" for models.
Tabular Data
Use cases: Fraud detection, medical diagnosis, customer churn prediction, loan approval
⚡ XGBoost
Supervised
Trains decision trees sequentially, each new tree corrects the errors of the previous one — using gradient descent to minimize a loss function. Regularization prevents overfitting.
Tabular Data
Use cases: Credit scoring, Kaggle competitions, click-through rate prediction, sales forecasting
🌿 Decision Tree
Supervised
Splits data into branches using yes/no questions on features, choosing splits that maximize information gain (reduce uncertainty). Produces a flowchart-like structure that is fully interpretable.
Tabular Data
Use cases: Medical triage, customer segmentation, rule-based business decisions
📐 Support Vector Machine
Supervised
Finds the hyperplane that maximizes the margin between two classes. Uses "kernel tricks" to handle non-linear boundaries by projecting data into higher-dimensional spaces.
Tabular Data NLP
Use cases: Text classification, image recognition, bioinformatics (gene classification)
🔵 K-Means Clustering
Unsupervised
Assigns data points to K clusters by iteratively updating cluster centers (centroids) to minimize the distance between each point and its nearest centroid until assignments stabilize.
Tabular Data
Use cases: Customer segmentation, document clustering, anomaly detection, image compression
📉 PCA
Unsupervised
Finds new axes (principal components) that capture the maximum variance in data. Projects high-dimensional data onto fewer dimensions while preserving as much information as possible.
Tabular Data Computer Vision
Use cases: Dimensionality reduction, noise filtering, data visualization, preprocessing for ML pipelines
👁️ CNN (ResNet / VGG)
Deep Learning
Uses convolutional filters to scan images and detect local patterns (edges, textures, shapes). Stacks layers to recognize increasingly complex features — from pixels to objects.
Computer Vision
Use cases: Image classification, object detection, medical imaging, autonomous vehicles, facial recognition
💬 BERT (Transformer)
Deep Learning
Reads text in both directions simultaneously using self-attention mechanisms to understand context. Pre-trained on massive text corpora, then fine-tuned on specific tasks with minimal data.
NLP
Use cases: Sentiment analysis, named entity recognition, question answering, document classification
✨ GPT (Generative Transformer)
Deep Learning
Predicts the next token in a sequence using a decoder-only transformer with masked self-attention. Trained on internet-scale text to generate fluent, coherent language.
Generative AI NLP
Use cases: Chatbots, code generation, content writing, summarization, translation
🎨 GAN (Generative Adversarial Network)
Deep Learning
Pits two neural networks against each other: a Generator creates fake samples, and a Discriminator tries to tell real from fake. Competing forces drive the generator to produce increasingly realistic outputs.
Generative AI Computer Vision
Use cases: Synthetic image generation, deepfakes, data augmentation, style transfer, drug discovery
🌊 Diffusion Model (DDPM)
Deep Learning
Gradually adds noise to training images until they become pure noise, then learns to reverse that process. At inference, starts from random noise and iteratively denoises to generate a new image.
Generative AI Computer Vision
Use cases: Text-to-image (Stable Diffusion, DALL·E), video generation, scientific image synthesis
🎮 Q-Learning (DQN)
Reinforcement
An agent learns by trial and error: it takes actions, receives rewards or penalties, and updates a Q-table (or deep network) to estimate the long-term value of each action in each state.
Reinforcement Learning
Use cases: Game playing (Atari, Chess), robotics, autonomous navigation, resource management

Value Proposition

Unique value

Unlike a simple list of algorithms, this framework shows the relationships between algorithms — how they cluster by learning paradigm, which domains they serve, and how they fit within the broader AI hierarchy. It reflects systems-level thinking, not just factual recall.

Relevance

Algorithm selection is one of the most consequential decisions in any ML project. Demonstrating the ability to compare, classify, and contextualize algorithms signals readiness for real-world ML engineering roles where choosing the right model is a core responsibility.

Artifact 02
Visualizing Neural Networks
An in-depth visual presentation defining and illustrating the core components of neural networks — layers, neurons, weights, activation functions, loss functions, and optimization algorithms — grounded in real TensorFlow Playground experiments.
Deep Learning Neural Networks TensorFlow Playground Classification Visualization

Description

A comprehensive visual presentation built around hands-on experimentation with the TensorFlow Neural Network Playground. The artifact covers all six required neural network components — layers, neurons, weights, activation functions, loss functions, and optimization algorithms — supported by an original architecture diagram, activation function comparisons, and three documented training experiments using Gaussian and Circle datasets.

Objective

To develop intuitive, visual understanding of how neural networks are structured and how they learn — by connecting abstract concepts (backpropagation, gradient descent, non-linearity) to observable, real-time behavior in the Playground. The experiments demonstrate how dataset type, feature selection, noise, and training epochs all affect model performance.

Process

  • Ran multiple experiments on TensorFlow Playground varying dataset, noise, features, and layers
  • Documented 3 key experiments: untrained baseline, converged Gaussian model, and circle dataset with feature engineering
  • Researched and defined each of the 6 neural network components with formulas and plain-language explanations
  • Designed an original SVG architecture diagram showing forward pass and backpropagation
  • Created activation function comparison visuals for Tanh, ReLU, Sigmoid, and Softmax
  • Built the complete visual presentation as a structured HTML portfolio artifact

Tools & Technologies

TensorFlow Playground HTML / CSS / SVG IBM Plex Mono Claude AI Classification · Tanh · LR 0.03

Network Architecture Diagram

Fig 1. Neural network architecture: 4 inputs → 2 hidden layers → 2 outputs. Connections carry learned weights w₁, w₂, w₃. Activation σ applied at every hidden neuron.

Neural Network Components — Definitions & Roles

COMPONENT 01

Layers

Layers are groups of neurons arranged in stages within a neural network. They help process information step-by-step as data moves through the model.

Types of Layers:

  • Input Layer: Receives the raw data, such as images or numerical values.
  • Hidden Layers: Perform calculations and detect patterns in the data.
  • Output Layer: Produces the final prediction or classification result.
Role: Layers allow the network to learn increasingly complex features from the input data.
COMPONENT 02

Neurons

Neurons are the basic computational units of a neural network. They function similarly to neurons in the human brain by receiving inputs, processing them, and generating outputs.

How Neurons Work:

  1. Receive input values.
  2. Multiply inputs by weights.
  3. Add a bias value.
  4. Apply an activation function.
  5. Produce an output.
Role: Neurons identify relationships and patterns in data. output = σ(Σ(wᵢxᵢ) + b)
COMPONENT 03

Weights

Weights are numerical values that determine the strength and importance of connections between neurons.

Role:
  • Large weights indicate stronger influence.
  • Small weights indicate weaker influence.

During training, the neural network continuously adjusts weights to improve prediction accuracy.

COMPONENT 04

Activation Functions

Activation functions are mathematical formulas that determine whether a neuron should activate and pass information forward.

Common Activation Functions:

  • Tanh: Produces outputs between -1 and 1.
  • ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit): Outputs positive values while ignoring negatives.
  • Sigmoid: Produces outputs between 0 and 1.
Role: Activation functions introduce nonlinearity, allowing neural networks to learn complex patterns and relationships.
COMPONENT 05

Loss Functions

Loss functions measure how far the model's predictions are from the correct answers.

Examples:

  • Mean Squared Error (MSE): Common in regression problems.
  • Cross-Entropy Loss: Common in classification problems.
Role: The loss function guides the network during training by showing how much error exists.
  • Low loss = better predictions.
  • High loss = poorer predictions.
COMPONENT 06

Optimization Algorithms

Optimization algorithms adjust the network's weights to minimize the loss function and improve performance.

Common Algorithms:

  • Gradient Descent
  • Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)
  • Adam Optimizer
Role: Optimization algorithms help the model learn efficiently by finding the best weight values for accurate predictions. w ← w - η · ∂L/∂w

Activation Function Comparison

Tanh
f(x) = tanh(x)
Outputs (−1,1). Zero-centered, smooth gradient.
✓ Used in experiments
ReLU
f(x) = max(0, x)
Most popular for hidden layers. Fast, reduces vanishing gradient.
Sigmoid
f(x) = 1/(1+e⁻ˣ)
Outputs (0,1). Used in binary output layers.
Softmax
σ(z)ᵢ = eᶻⁱ/Σeᶻʲ
Probability distribution summing to 1. Multi-class output.

Training Process: How the Network Learns

📥
Step 1 — Forward Pass

Input features (X₁, X₂ from Playground) flow through each layer. Each neuron applies tanh(Σwᵢxᵢ + b) and passes its output forward. The final layer produces a prediction ŷ.

📊
Step 2 — Loss Calculation

The prediction is compared to the true label. In Experiment 1 (epoch 0), loss = 1.584 — the model was guessing. The loss function quantifies exactly how wrong the current weights are.

🔄
Step 3 — Backpropagation

The chain rule is applied in reverse to compute how much each weight contributed to the loss (∂L/∂w). This gives each weight a gradient — direction and magnitude for improvement.

✅
Step 4 — Weight Update (SGD, lr = 0.03)

Each weight is nudged: w ← w − 0.03 · ∂L/∂w. After 284 epochs (Experiment 2), loss reached 0.000. The loop repeats every epoch until convergence.

TensorFlow Playground Experiments

EXP 01
Gaussian Dataset · 0 Hidden Layers · Epoch 0 — Before Training
DatasetGaussian (2 clusters)
Hidden Layers0
FeaturesX₁ and X₂
ActivationTanh
Noise0
Epochs000,000
Test Loss1.584
Training Loss1.622
Observation: Before training, random weights produce an undefined boundary and high loss (1.5+). The output shows an unfocused gradient — demonstrating that untrained weights produce meaningless predictions.
EXP 02
Gaussian Dataset · 0 Hidden Layers · 284 Epochs · Noise = 5 · Converged
DatasetGaussian (2 clusters)
Hidden Layers0 (linear model)
FeaturesX₁ and X₂
ActivationTanh
Noise5
Epochs000,284
Test Loss0.000
Training Loss0.003
Observation: After 284 epochs, loss converged to ~0. A sharp diagonal boundary cleanly separates both clusters. Confirms the Gaussian dataset is linearly separable — no hidden layers needed when raw X₁/X₂ features are sufficient.
EXP 03
Circle Dataset · 0 Hidden Layers · X₁² & X₂² Features · 127 Epochs
DatasetCircle (non-linear)
Hidden Layers0
FeaturesX₁² and X₂²
ActivationTanh
Noise0
Epochs000,127
Test Loss0.004
Training Loss0.003
Observation: Raw X₁/X₂ cannot separate a circle — but X₁² and X₂² transform the feature space so a linear boundary becomes a circle in the original space. Near-zero loss in 127 epochs proves that feature engineering can replace hidden layers.

Summary & Key Insights

Why Visualizing Neural Networks Matters

Visualizing neural networks bridges the gap between abstract mathematics and intuitive understanding. By watching the Playground in real time — loss dropping, boundaries sharpening, weights strengthening — the mechanics of machine learning become tangible rather than theoretical.

📉
Loss Measures Learning

Loss dropped from 1.584 (random) to 0.000 after 284 epochs. Watching loss fall confirms optimization algorithms are working.

🧱
Match Depth to Complexity

Gaussian needed 0 layers; circle also needed 0 — with the right features. Deeper networks are for more complex datasets, not all datasets.

🔀
Non-Linearity is Essential

Activation functions give networks expressive power beyond linear models. Without them, stacked layers solve nothing new.

🎯
Feature Engineering = Power

X₁²/X₂² solved the circle without any hidden layers. Thoughtful feature selection can replace network depth.

⚖️
Weights Encode Knowledge

All intelligence lives in learned weight values — adjusted iteratively via backpropagation to reduce loss.

🎲
Noise Tests Generalization

Experiment 2 used noise=5 yet converged — because the Gaussian problem is inherently simple and linearly separable.

Value Proposition

Unique value

This artifact connects mathematical concepts — gradient descent, non-linearity, backpropagation — directly to observable experimental outcomes. The three Playground experiments provide empirical evidence for each theoretical claim, demonstrating both conceptual depth and scientific thinking.

Relevance

Neural networks are the foundation of modern AI. Demonstrating clear, visual understanding of how they are structured and trained — including the role of features, activations, loss, and optimization — signals readiness for deep learning engineering roles and ML research environments.

Artifact 03
Plant Disease Detection (CNN)
A convolutional neural network fine-tuned on 54,000 plant leaf images to classify 38 disease categories, then deployed as a lightweight web application for use by smallholder farmers in the field.
Deep Learning Computer Vision Transfer Learning Deployment

Description

A computer vision project fine-tuning ResNet-50 (pretrained on ImageNet) on the PlantVillage dataset to identify 38 crop disease categories across 14 plant species. The model achieves 94.2% validation accuracy. Packaged as a Flask web app with a mobile-responsive UI users upload a leaf photo and receive an instant diagnosis and treatment recommendation.

Objective

To demonstrate end-to-end deep learning development from dataset preparation and transfer learning through model evaluation and real-world deployment while applying computer vision to an agricultural problem with genuine social impact potential in food security.

Process

  • Downloaded and preprocessed the PlantVillage dataset (54,305 images across 38 classes)
  • Applied data augmentation: random flips, rotations, color jitter, and Gaussian blur
  • Fine-tuned ResNet-50 with frozen early layers; unfroze and fine-tuned final 3 blocks
  • Used learning rate scheduling (cosine annealing) and early stopping
  • Evaluated with per-class precision/recall to identify low-performing disease categories
  • Exported model to ONNX for efficient inference; built Flask API with image upload endpoint
  • Designed and deployed responsive front-end with real-time prediction display

Tools & Technologies

PythonPyTorch torchvisionResNet-50 AlbumentationsONNX FlaskHTML/CSS/JS

Value Proposition

Unique value

This artifact demonstrates the complete deep learning lifecycle: dataset curation, transfer learning strategy, rigorous evaluation, model optimization, and web deployment. The social impact framing (food security, smallholder farmers) shows awareness of why we build AI, not just how.

Relevance

Computer vision is one of the fastest-growing areas of applied AI. Transfer learning with pretrained CNNs is the dominant paradigm used by industry teams. Deploying the model as an accessible web app shows the ability to take a model from notebook to production — a critical gap many ML students can't bridge.

Artifact 04
Sentiment Analysis Pipeline (BERT)
An end-to-end NLP pipeline fine-tuning BERT on 50,000 product reviews for five-class sentiment classification, served via a FastAPI microservice with a real-time inference endpoint.
Deep Learning NLP Transformers API Deployment

Description

Fine-tuned bert-base-uncased on the Amazon product review dataset for 5-class sentiment classification (1–5 stars). The training pipeline includes dynamic padding, mixed-precision training, and gradient accumulation for efficient GPU use. Deployed as a FastAPI service with batch inference support, achieving 88.4% accuracy and sub-200ms response time.

Objective

To gain hands-on mastery of the transformer fine-tuning workflow using HuggingFace Transformers, while building a production-grade NLP service that handles real-world text inputs — demonstrating both model development and MLOps competency.

Process

  • Loaded and cleaned 50,000 Amazon reviews; performed EDA on label distribution and text length
  • Tokenized with bert-base-uncased tokenizer using dynamic padding and truncation
  • Fine-tuned with AdamW optimizer, linear warmup schedule, and mixed-precision (fp16)
  • Tracked experiments with Weights & Biases; compared BERT vs. DistilBERT vs. RoBERTa
  • Applied LIME to understand model attention on ambiguous reviews
  • Wrapped model in FastAPI with async inference, input validation, and batch endpoints
  • Containerized with Docker; documented API with auto-generated OpenAPI/Swagger spec

Tools & Technologies

PythonHuggingFace Transformers BERT / DistilBERTPyTorch FastAPIDocker Weights & BiasesLIME

Value Proposition

Unique value

This artifact goes beyond fine-tuning a BERT model to include experiment tracking, model comparison, explainability, and containerized deployment a complete MLOps workflow that reflects how NLP systems are actually built and maintained in industry.

Relevance

NLP and transformer models are the most in-demand technical skill in AI/ML hiring right now. Employers specifically look for HuggingFace experience and the ability to deploy models as services. This artifact directly addresses those requirements, demonstrating both the research and engineering sides of modern NLP.

✨
Artifact 05
Generative AI Exploration: Image Synthesis with GANs & Diffusion
A comparative study of two generative AI paradigms GANs and Diffusion Models, implemented and evaluated on the MNIST and CIFAR-10 datasets, with analysis of trade-offs in image quality, training stability, and generation diversity.
Deep Learning Generative AI Computer Vision Research

Description

A research-style project implementing both a DCGAN (Deep Convolutional GAN) and a simplified DDPM (Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model) from scratch in PyTorch. Both were trained on MNIST (handwritten digits) and CIFAR-10 (natural images). The report compares Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) scores, training dynamics, mode collapse behavior, and sample diversity, with discussion of each paradigm's strengths and limitations.

Objective

To develop a first-principles understanding of the two dominant generative AI paradigms in computer vision — GANs and diffusion models — by implementing both from scratch and conducting a rigorous empirical comparison. The goal is to understand why diffusion models have largely superseded GANs, and in what scenarios each remains preferable.

Process

  • Implemented DCGAN: generator/discriminator architecture, training loop with label smoothing, gradient penalty for training stability
  • Implemented DDPM: forward diffusion (noise scheduling), UNet denoising architecture, DDIM sampling for faster inference
  • Trained both models on MNIST (60k images) and CIFAR-10 (50k images) for 100 epochs each
  • Evaluated using FID scores, Inception Score, and visual inspection of sample diversity
  • Documented GAN failure modes: mode collapse, training instability, oscillating losses
  • Wrote a structured comparative report: architecture, training dynamics, sample quality, use-case recommendations

Tools & Technologies

PythonPyTorch DCGANDDPM / DDIM torchvisionFID evaluation Google Colab (A100)Weights & Biases

Value Proposition

Unique value

Implementing both architectures from scratch rather than calling a high-level API demonstrates genuine mathematical and architectural understanding of generative AI. The comparative analysis with quantitative metrics (FID) shows research-level rigor and the ability to critically evaluate model trade-offs, not just report best-case results.

Relevance

Generative AI is the fastest-growing segment of the AI industry, with applications in content creation, drug discovery, material science, and synthetic data generation. Having hands-on experience with both GANs and diffusion models positions me at the frontier of applied AI research and directly aligns with roles at AI labs, creative AI companies, and research teams.

📉
Workshop 3
3.3 Training Approaches
A structured exploration of supervised vs. unsupervised learning, loss functions, parameter tuning, and overfitting/underfitting — grounded in nine hands-on TensorFlow Playground simulation experiments on the XOR dataset varying architecture depth and width.
Supervised Learning Unsupervised Learning Neural Networks Loss Functions TensorFlow Playground Parameter Tuning

Description

A comprehensive academic paper and simulation study covering core ML training concepts: supervised vs. unsupervised learning, loss function behavior (MSE, Cross-Entropy), parameter tuning (learning rate, neuron count, layers, activation functions, regularization), and the overfitting/underfitting spectrum — backed by nine documented TensorFlow Playground experiments on the XOR classification dataset.

Objective

To develop an empirical, hands-on understanding of how neural network architecture choices and hyperparameter settings directly affect model performance — connecting theoretical concepts (capacity, generalization, loss minimization) to observable, real-time results in TF Playground.

Process

  • Reviewed supervised vs. unsupervised paradigms and their evaluation methods
  • Studied loss functions: MSE for regression, Cross-Entropy for classification
  • Ran experiments on TF Playground: XOR dataset, 50/50 train/test split, LR = 0.03, Tanh activation
  • Systematically varied hidden layers (0–2) and neuron counts to observe underfitting/adequate/good fit
  • Documented training loss, test loss, and epoch count for each of nine configurations
  • Compiled results into a comparative summary table and written analysis

Tools & Technologies

TensorFlow Playground XOR Dataset Tanh Activation Classification Task Indiana Wesleyan University

1 · Theoretical Background

1.1 Supervised Learning

Trains a model on labeled data where each input has a known correct output. The model minimizes the difference between predictions and true labels.

  • Data: labeled (input + correct output)
  • Goal: predict output for new inputs
  • Examples: Classification, Regression
  • Evaluation: clear via loss / accuracy
1.2 Unsupervised Learning

Uses unlabeled data — the model must find hidden structure on its own with no predefined correct answers.

  • Data: unlabeled (input only)
  • Goal: discover patterns/groupings
  • Examples: K-Means, PCA
  • Evaluation: harder to measure objectively
1.3 Loss Functions

Measures how wrong predictions are. The optimizer minimizes this by adjusting weights — lower loss = better predictions.

  • MSE — regression; penalizes large errors heavily
  • Cross-Entropy — classification; measures probability divergence
  • TF Playground shows train & test loss in real-time
1.4 Parameter Tuning & Fitting

Adjusting settings that control how a model learns. Overfitting: low train loss, high test loss. Underfitting: both losses remain high.

  • Learning Rate — step size per update
  • Neuron Count — model capacity
  • Hidden Layers — hierarchical features
  • Activation — non-linearity (Tanh, ReLU)
  • Regularization — prevent overfitting (L1/L2)
Supervised Unsupervised
DataLabeled (input + correct output)Unlabeled (input only)
GoalPredict output for new inputsDiscover patterns/groupings
ExamplesClassification, RegressionClustering (K-Means), PCA
EvaluationClear — loss / accuracyHarder to measure objectively

2 · Experiment A — Network Architecture (Hidden Layers & Neurons)

All experiments: XOR dataset · 50/50 train/test split · LR = 0.03 · Tanh activation · Classification

UNDERFITTING
0 Hidden Layers — Linear classifier cannot learn non-linear XOR boundary
Config0 hidden layers
Test loss0.001 *
Training loss0.000 *
Epoch153
Observation: Raw X1, X2 inputs only — simple linear classifier that cannot learn the non-linear XOR boundary. *Low loss misleading due to accidental linear separation, not genuine learning. Epoch 153.
UNDERFITTING
1 Hidden Layer, 1 Neuron — Lacks capacity to form a meaningful non-linear boundary
Config1 layer · 1 neuron
Test loss0.399
Training loss0.378
Epoch757
Observation: Both losses ~0.38–0.40 and high. The single neuron in the hidden layer still lacks capacity for a meaningful non-linear boundary. Model cannot capture the XOR pattern. Epoch 757.
WEAK FIT
1 Hidden Layer, 2 Neurons — Curved but imprecise diagonal boundary begins to form
Config1 layer · 2 neurons
Test loss0.211
Training loss0.180
Epoch438
Observation: A second neuron provides slightly more capacity — a diagonal-ish boundary begins to form. Test loss 0.211, Training loss 0.180 after 438 epochs. Still not accurate enough, but better than 1 neuron.
ADEQUATE
1 Hidden Layer, 4 Neurons — Standard baseline; recognizable rectangular XOR boundary
Config1 layer · 4 neurons
Test loss0.140
Training loss0.069
Epoch1,692
Observation: Four neurons provide adequate capacity. A recognizable rectangular decision boundary emerges. Standard baseline configuration — Test loss 0.140, Training loss 0.069. Epoch 1,692.
GOOD FIT
1 Hidden Layer, 8 Neurons — Sharp XOR boundary; major improvement with faster convergence
Config1 layer · 8 neurons
Test loss0.021
Training loss0.004
Epoch493
Observation: Eight neurons give significantly more representational power. The XOR boundary becomes sharp and accurate. Test loss 0.021, Training loss 0.004 — major improvement over 4 neurons, converged much faster (493 vs. 1,692 epochs).

3 · Experiment B — Increasing Network Depth (2 Hidden Layers)

POOR
2 Hidden Layers, 2+1 Neurons — Technically deep but under-parameterized
Config2 layers · 2+1 neurons
Test loss0.222
Training loss0.179
Epoch679
Observation: Adding a second layer with only 2→1 neurons still produces a poor result. Technically deep but under-parameterized. Depth alone is not enough — worse than 1 layer with 8 neurons. Epoch 679.
STRUGGLING
2 Hidden Layers, 2+2 Neurons — Network depth alone does not compensate for insufficient neurons
Config2 layers · 2+2 neurons
Test loss0.298
Training loss0.265
Epoch760
Observation: Slightly more capacity with 2+2 but still underperforming — test loss 0.298, training loss 0.265 after 760 epochs. Network depth alone does not compensate for insufficient neurons per layer.
EXCELLENT
2 Hidden Layers, 3+2 Neurons — Fastest convergence & sharpest boundary across all experiments
Config2 layers · 3+2 neurons
Test loss0.007
Training loss0.002
Epoch238
Observation: Increasing to 3 neurons in layer 1 and 2 in layer 2 produces an excellent result. The XOR boundary is sharp and correct. Test loss 0.007, Training loss 0.002 — fastest convergence (238 epochs) across all experiments.
BEST OVERALL
2 Hidden Layers, 4+2 Neurons — Best balance of accuracy and generalization
Config2 layers · 4+2 neurons
Test loss0.012
Training loss0.001
Epoch340
Observation: Best balance of accuracy and generalization. Test loss 0.012, Training loss 0.001 at epoch 340. Adding the second layer with adequate neurons allows more efficient hierarchical feature learning than a single wider layer.

4 · Conclusion — Summary of All Configurations

Configuration Result Test Loss Train Loss Key Observation
0 hidden layersFailed0.001*0.000*Cannot learn non-linear patterns — needs hidden layers
1 layer, 1 neuronUnderfitting0.3990.378Loss ~0.40 — too simple, no meaningful boundary formed
1 layer, 2 neuronsWeak0.2110.180Some learning but loss still ~0.21 — insufficient capacity
1 layer, 4 neuronsAdequate0.1400.069Baseline fit — recognizable boundary, loss ~0.14/0.07
1 layer, 8 neuronsGood0.0210.004Sharp boundary, fast convergence — loss drops to 0.021
2 layers, 2+1Poor0.2220.179Deep but under-parameterized — depth alone not enough
2 layers, 2+2Struggling0.2980.265Still struggling to define XOR boundary
2 layers, 3+2Excellent0.0070.002Fastest convergence (238 epochs), test loss 0.007
2 layers, 4+2Best0.0120.001Best generalization — test 0.012, training 0.001

Both depth (number of layers) and width (neurons per layer) must be considered together. A deep network with too few neurons underperforms a shallow but wider network. The sweet spot — 2 layers with 3–4 neurons in the first layer and 2 in the second — consistently produced the best results. These experiments confirm that supervised learning's power lies in having a measurable loss signal guiding training. Parameter tuning, especially architecture design, is an iterative process of balancing model capacity against task complexity.

Value Proposition

Unique value

This artifact empirically demonstrates how architecture choices — depth and width — must be considered together. The nine systematic experiments move beyond textbook definitions to provide evidence-based conclusions grounded in real simulation data, showing underfitting, adequate fit, and generalization with concrete loss numbers.

Relevance

Parameter tuning and architecture design are daily tasks for ML engineers. The ability to systematically vary configurations, observe loss dynamics, and interpret underfitting vs. overfitting signals is a foundational competency that directly maps to professional model development with TensorFlow, PyTorch, or any modern framework.

🔍
Workshop 4
4.4 Explainable AI & Large Language Models
A visual reference and explanatory document mapping the landscape of Explainable AI (XAI) for LLMs — covering the core definition, the structural challenges that make billion-parameter models hard to interpret, the metrics used to validate model trustworthiness, and the current techniques and industry efforts working to close the transparency gap.
Explainable AI LLM Interpretability AI Ethics & Policy Mechanistic Interpretability Model Evaluation Regulatory Compliance

Description

A one-page visual reference plus written explanatory document that maps Explainable AI (XAI) across four interconnected dimensions: what it is and why it matters, the structural challenges that make large language models opaque, the metrics researchers use to validate model reliability and safety, and the current techniques — from attention visualization to mechanistic interpretability — being developed to close the gap.

Objective

To build a clear, evidence-grounded understanding of why state-of-the-art LLMs (GPT, Claude, Gemini, LLaMA) remain difficult to interpret despite their capabilities, and to survey the validation metrics, interpretability methods, and governance tools the field is using to build trust, reliability, and accountability into deployed AI systems.

Process

  • Researched the definition of XAI and why transparency matters for trust, accountability, and safety in high-stakes domains (healthcare, law, finance, hiring)
  • Identified six structural challenges that make LLMs hard to explain — from black-box opacity to dynamic internal state
  • Surveyed accuracy/benchmark metrics (MMLU, TruthfulQA, HumanEval) and safety/alignment metrics (red-teaming, bias audits, RLHF scoring) used to validate models independent of their ability to explain themselves
  • Catalogued current interpretability techniques and governance tools — attention visualization, probing classifiers, chain-of-thought prompting, circuit analysis, model cards, RLHF & Constitutional AI
  • Mapped which industry leaders (Anthropic, Google DeepMind, Meta AI, OpenAI, Hugging Face, IBM) are active in each area of XAI research
  • Synthesized the findings into a one-page visual reference graphic and a written explanatory document summarizing the core tension, key considerations, and design rationale

Tools & Technologies

BertViz TransformerLens Hugging Face Leaderboards Constitutional AI Indiana Wesleyan University

1 · What Is Explainable AI?

Definition

Explainable AI (XAI) — a set of methods and processes that allow humans to understand, trust, and appropriately oversee the outputs of AI systems, including why a model produced a given result, not just what the result was.

Why It Matters

Trust, accountability, safety — as LLMs are deployed in healthcare, law, finance, and hiring, understanding their reasoning is critical for catching errors, auditing decisions, and meeting regulatory requirements.

Parameters (GPT-4 est.)
~1 Trillion
non-linear interactions
Key AI models
GPT · Claude · Gemini · LLaMA
share opacity challenges
XAI maturity
Emerging
no single solution exists yet
Regulatory push
EU AI Act
mandates explainability for high-risk AI

2 · Key Challenges in Explainability

? Black-box opacity

Decisions emerge from billions of parameters with no single interpretable component — the model's reasoning is distributed and invisible across the network.

! Post-hoc gap

Explanations are generated after the decision and may not faithfully reflect the model's actual internal process — they can be plausible but misleading.

# Data quality & bias

Opaque training data introduces biases that are difficult to detect or trace, compounding the difficulty of interpreting model outputs fairly.

~ Scale & non-linearity

Non-linear interactions across layers resist straightforward interpretation — complexity grows as models become larger and more capable.

§ Regulatory pressure

Compliance frameworks like the EU AI Act demand documentation and auditability that current LLM architectures are not natively designed to provide.

≈ Dynamic internal state

Model weights are static but contextual attention shifts with every input — making consistent explanation across sessions difficult to achieve.

3 · Validation & Performance Metrics

These are the tools researchers and deployers use to assess whether a model is reliable, safe, and accountable — independent of whether it can explain itself.

Accuracy & Benchmark Metrics
  • MMLU — knowledge breadth
  • HellaSwag — commonsense reasoning
  • TruthfulQA — factual accuracy
  • HumanEval — code generation
  • BLEU / ROUGE — text generation quality
  • F1, precision, recall — classification
Safety & Alignment Metrics
  • Red-teaming pass rate
  • Refusal accuracy on harmful prompts
  • Bias audits (BBQ, WinoBias)
  • Constitutional AI evaluations
  • RLHF reward model scoring
  • Calibration — Expected Calibration Error (ECE)
How Metrics Build Trust → Reliability → Accountability
Step 1
Benchmark on held-out test sets
Step 2
Human evaluation & red-teaming
Step 3
Ongoing monitoring in deployment
Step 4
Audit trail & model cards published

4 · Current Solutions & Techniques

Interpretability method
Attention visualization

Heatmaps and saliency maps show which tokens an attention head focuses on. Tools like BertViz and TransformerLens expose multi-head attention patterns — a window into what the model attends to, even if not why.

Interpretability method
Probing classifiers

Lightweight classifiers trained on hidden states reveal what linguistic or factual knowledge is encoded in specific layers — mapping internal representations to human-readable concepts.

Prompting technique
Chain-of-thought prompting

Encouraging the model to think step by step externalizes intermediate reasoning, making it easier to audit logic, catch errors early, and improve factual consistency on multi-step tasks.

Mechanistic interpretability
Circuit analysis (Anthropic)

Anthropic's research identifies circuits — subgraphs of attention heads and MLP neurons — that implement specific behaviors such as indirect object identification or factual recall.

Policy & governance
Model cards & system cards

Standardized documentation (pioneered by Google, adopted by Meta and Anthropic) describes intended use, known limitations, bias evaluations, and training data provenance.

Alignment research
RLHF & Constitutional AI

Reinforcement learning from human feedback shapes model behavior toward human preferences. Constitutional AI introduces principles the model uses to self-critique, reducing reliance on opaque fine-tuning signals.

Industry Leaders Active in XAI Research
Anthropic — mechanistic interpretability, Constitutional AI Google DeepMind — Gemini eval suites, TCAV probing Meta AI — LLaMA model cards, OPT transparency reports
OpenAI — GPT-4 system card, scalable oversight Hugging Face — open evaluation leaderboards IBM — AI Fairness 360, AI Explainability 360

5 · Explanatory Document

The graphic above maps the landscape of Explainable AI (XAI) across four interconnected dimensions. At its core is a fundamental tension: large language models like GPT-4, Claude, Gemini, and LLaMA derive their remarkable capabilities from scale — billions of parameters interacting in non-linear, distributed ways — yet that same scale is precisely what makes their decision-making so difficult to interpret. One of the quieter problems in AI transparency is that explanations generated after the fact can sound convincing without actually reflecting what the model did internally, a gap researchers call the post-hoc problem. On top of that, training data is often opaque enough that the biases baked into it are genuinely difficult to trace back to their source. Add regulations like the EU AI Act pushing organizations to audit systems that were never built with auditability in mind, and the challenge becomes structural — these are not early-stage growing pains that will sort themselves out as models improve; they are fundamental properties of how neural networks work, and scaling up is unlikely to make them easier to solve.

On the response side, the field hasn't been static. Researchers can see at least a portion of what a model is encoding thanks to attention visualization and probing classifiers. Because chain-of-thought prompting highlights intermediate reasoning, mistakes can be identified before they become more serious. Mechanistic interpretability goes deeper still — trying to identify the specific neuron circuits behind particular behaviors, essentially reverse-engineering the model from the inside out. On the process side, tools like model cards, Constitutional AI, and RLHF build accountability into how models get deployed and fine-tuned. Red-teaming and bias audits, along with benchmark suites like MMLU and TruthfulQA, provide an empirical basis for the entire endeavor.

The honest assessment is that this is a serious, multi-front research initiative making significant progress against a truly difficult problem — even though none of it adds up to complete openness just yet.

Key consideration

Scale is both the source of LLM capability and the source of its opacity — these two properties cannot be cleanly separated.

Design rationale

The graphic groups challenges, metrics, and solutions into parallel visual blocks so a reader can map each problem directly to the techniques addressing it.

Main takeaway

XAI is an active, multi-front research effort — real progress exists, but no single technique delivers full transparency yet.

Value Proposition

Unique value

This artifact demonstrates the ability to take a genuinely difficult, fast-moving research area and distill it into a structured, audience-ready visual reference — connecting the technical (circuit analysis, probing classifiers) to the organizational (model cards, regulatory compliance) without losing rigor.

Relevance

Explainability is becoming a hard requirement, not a nice-to-have — the EU AI Act and similar frameworks now mandate it for high-risk systems. Understanding the interpretability toolkit and validation pipeline is directly relevant to any ML role that touches deployment, auditing, or responsible AI practice.

📊
Workshop 5
Selecting Pre-Trained Models Across Domains
A trade-off analysis and decision matrix for choosing pre-trained models across NLP/GenAI, Computer Vision, and Tabular domains — balancing size, accuracy, speed, and interpretability against real deployment constraints.
NLP / GenAI Computer Vision Tabular Model Selection

1. Introduction

Modern AI systems are rarely built from scratch. Practitioners assemble them from pre-trained models, each carrying its own profile of capability and cost. The dominant tension in this choice is between raw performance and the practical constraints of deployment: a model that is larger and more accurate is also slower, more expensive to run, and frequently harder to understand.

Explainability deserves special emphasis because it is unevenly distributed across domains. Tree-based tabular models can be interrogated feature by feature, while large language and deep vision models behave largely as black boxes whose internal reasoning is opaque. In regulated settings (credit, healthcare, hiring), an unexplainable model may be unusable regardless of its accuracy. Selecting a model therefore means balancing four competing pressures simultaneously — size, accuracy, speed, and interpretability — against the requirements of the specific application and domain.

2. Methodology

Six models were chosen to span the three domains named in the brief, with two representatives each to expose intra-domain trade-offs:

  • NLP / Generative AI: a GPT-4-class generative model and BERT-base, contrasting generation against understanding.
  • Computer Vision: EfficientNet-B7 and MobileNetV3, contrasting accuracy-optimized against mobile-optimized designs.
  • Tabular: XGBoost and LightGBM, the two dominant gradient-boosting frameworks for structured data.

Data on parameter counts, benchmark accuracy, and inference characteristics were gathered from the original architecture papers (BERT, MobileNet, EfficientNet), published benchmark leaderboards (GLUE, ImageNet), and recent comparative studies of gradient-boosting libraries. Figures are reported as representative ranges rather than single point values, since exact numbers vary with hardware, quantization, and configuration. Each model was then scored on a three-level scale (favorable / moderate / costly) per criterion to populate the decision matrix.

3. Decision Matrix

The matrix below compares all six models across the four core criteria. Cell color encodes how favorable each model is on that dimension — green is best, red signals the steepest cost or constraint.

Model Domain Model Size Accuracy Speed Interpretability
GPT-4 class (generative) NLP / GenAI Very large (est. ~1T+ MoE params) SOTA generation & reasoning; ~86% MMLU High latency; needs GPU clusters Black box; low
BERT-base NLP (understanding) 110M params (~440 MB) GLUE ~79.6 avg Fast; CPU-feasible after fine-tune Attention probing; low–moderate
EfficientNet-B7 Computer Vision 66M params ~84.4% ImageNet top-1 Slower than mobile nets Saliency / Grad-CAM only; low
MobileNetV3 Computer Vision ~3–5M params ~75% ImageNet top-1 Real-time on mobile CPU Saliency only; low
XGBoost Tabular Small (MBs; tree ensemble) Top-tier on structured data Fast train & inference SHAP + feature importance; high
LightGBM Tabular Small (MBs; tree ensemble) Comparable to XGBoost 11–15× faster on large sets SHAP support; high
Legend: High / Favorable Moderate Low / Costly

Reading the matrix. Two patterns stand out. First, the tabular models (XGBoost, LightGBM) are favorable on nearly every axis — small, fast, accurate on structured data, and uniquely transparent — which is why they remain default choices outside of perceptual tasks. Second, capability in NLP and vision is bought with size and opacity: the GPT-4-class model leads on accuracy but is the most costly to run and the least interpretable, while lightweight options (MobileNetV3, BERT-base) recover speed and deployability by accepting a ceiling on accuracy.

4. Analysis & Recommendations

4.1 NLP / Generative AI

GPT-4-class models deliver state-of-the-art generation and reasoning but at enormous parameter scale, high latency, GPU-cluster cost, and essentially no interpretability. BERT-base (110M parameters, ~79.6 average GLUE score) is far cheaper, fine-tunes on modest hardware, and serves classification or extraction tasks at low latency.

Recommendation: reserve large generative models for open-ended generation, complex reasoning, and zero-shot tasks where their capability is decisive. For bounded understanding tasks — sentiment, classification, entity extraction, search ranking — a fine-tuned BERT (or a distilled variant such as DistilBERT at 66M parameters) is more economical and easier to operate at scale.

4.2 Computer Vision

EfficientNet-B7 reaches roughly 84% ImageNet top-1 accuracy through compound scaling, making it a strong choice when accuracy is paramount and a server GPU is available. MobileNetV3 trades a few points of accuracy (~75% top-1) for a tiny footprint (3–5M parameters) and real-time inference on mobile CPUs. Neither offers strong interpretability beyond saliency methods such as Grad-CAM.

Recommendation: choose EfficientNet (or a smaller B0–B3 variant) for cloud or batch classification where accuracy dominates; choose MobileNetV3 for on-device, latency-sensitive, or power-constrained deployments. EfficientNet-Lite variants bridge the gap, offering quantized models that run near real-time on mobile while retaining most of the accuracy.

4.3 Tabular Data

XGBoost and LightGBM are close in accuracy on structured data, with XGBoost often marginally ahead on small-to-medium datasets and offering forgiving defaults, while LightGBM's leaf-wise, histogram-based growth makes it dramatically faster (reported 11–15x in some benchmarks) on large, high-cardinality data. Both expose feature importance and integrate cleanly with SHAP, giving them the interpretability that deep models lack.

Recommendation: default to XGBoost for small-to-medium datasets, regulated review cycles, or when forgiving defaults matter; switch to LightGBM for large datasets, tight training budgets, or memory-constrained pipelines. Because their scikit-learn-compatible APIs are nearly interchangeable, teams can prototype with one and switch with a one-line change. Where interpretability is a hard requirement, gradient-boosted trees should be preferred over any deep model.

5. Conclusion

No single model wins across all criteria; the right choice is the one whose trade-off profile matches the deployment context. Tabular gradient-boosting models are favorable almost everywhere they apply and should be the default for structured data, especially where explanations are required. In perceptual and language domains, accuracy is purchased with size, cost, and opacity — so the practical question becomes how much accuracy a given latency, hardware, and interpretability budget can afford.

The broader implication is that model selection is an engineering decision, not merely a leaderboard lookup. Teams should start from their constraints — latency targets, hardware, regulatory exposure, and the need to explain decisions — and select the smallest, most transparent model that clears the required accuracy bar, escalating to larger black-box models only when the task genuinely demands their capability.

Tools & Technologies

GPT-4 class BERT-base EfficientNet-B7 MobileNetV3 XGBoost LightGBM SHAP Grad-CAM GLUE / ImageNet benchmarks

Value Proposition

Unique value

Rather than chasing the top of a single leaderboard, this artifact reasons about four competing pressures at once and makes the trade-offs explicit in a single decision matrix. It demonstrates the systems-level judgment employers actually need: matching a model's trade-off profile to a deployment context, and knowing when a smaller, transparent model beats a larger black box.

Relevance

Modern AI systems are assembled from pre-trained models, not built from scratch. Choosing well — under real constraints of latency, cost, hardware, and regulatory explainability — is a core MLOps and applied-ML skill, directly relevant to roles where deployment and governance matter as much as raw accuracy.
